note: Dates are approximate. Biblical chronology is debated among scholars. This timeline follows a broadly evangelical framework as a baseline.

events.title: Creation
events.description: The creation of the heavens and earth as described in Genesis 1–2.
events.dateRationale: Traditional chronology based on the Masoretic Text genealogies places creation around 4004 BC (Ussher) or 4000 BC (various). Modern evangelical scholars generally treat the Genesis days as periods rather than literal 24-hour days, making precise dating impossible.
events.debate: Young-earth creationists follow Ussher's 4004 BC. Old-earth creationists and most mainstream scholars regard the Genesis creation account as theological rather than chronological, making any specific date a theological rather than historical calculation.

sources.label: John Walton — The Lost World of Genesis One

perspectives.label: Young Earth Creationism
perspectives.summary: The universe and life were created in six literal 24-hour days, approximately 6,000–10,000 years ago. All apparent evidence for an old earth is attributed to the global Flood or flawed dating methods.

perspectives.label: Old Earth / Progressive Creationism
perspectives.summary: The universe is billions of years old as science indicates, but God directly created distinct life forms at various points in history. The Genesis 'days' are long ages. Hugh Ross argues the Big Bang itself is evidence for a Creator.

sources.label: Ross — The Creator and the Cosmos

perspectives.label: Intelligent Design
perspectives.summary: Certain features of living organisms — particularly the information content of DNA, and 'irreducibly complex' molecular machines — are best explained by an intelligent cause rather than undirected natural processes. ID proponents argue this is a scientific rather than religious claim.
thinkers: Stephen Meyer (Discovery Institute) — Signature in the Cell

sources.label: Discovery Institute — Center for Science and Culture

perspectives.label: Theistic Evolution
perspectives.summary: Evolution is the mechanism God used to create life. There is no conflict between evolutionary science and Christian faith — God works through natural processes. The Genesis creation accounts are theological rather than scientific texts.
thinkers: Francis Collins — led the Human Genome Project, founder of BioLogos

sources.label: Polkinghorne — Belief in God in an Age of Science

perspectives.label: Fine-Tuning and Cosmic Design
perspectives.summary: The fundamental constants of physics appear exquisitely calibrated to permit complex life — a phenomenon known as the 'fine-tuned universe'. Some physicists see this as pointing toward purpose or intelligence; others invoke the multiverse as an explanation.
thinkers: Roger Penrose (Nobel Prize) — extraordinary low entropy of the Big Bang
George Ellis — philosophical implications of cosmology

sources.label: Lennox — God's Undertaker: Has Science Buried God?
Ellis — On the Moral Nature of the Universe

perspectives.label: Science and Faith — Compatibility
perspectives.summary: A broader perspective: many leading scientists across history and today hold religious belief alongside rigorous scientific practice. The perceived conflict between science and faith is argued by historians to be a 19th century invention rather than an inherent tension.
thinkers: John Lennox (Oxford mathematician and Christian apologist)

sources.label: Numbers — Galileo Goes to Jail (myth vs reality of science/faith conflict)
Faraday Institute for Science and Religion — Cambridge

events.title: Noah and the Flood
events.description: The great flood and God's covenant with Noah, symbolised by the rainbow (Genesis 6–9).
events.dateRationale: Based on Masoretic genealogies in Genesis 5 and 11. Ussher dated the flood to 2349 BC. The Septuagint genealogies produce a significantly later date around 3000 BC, used here as a round figure.
events.debate: The historicity and universality of the flood is one of the most debated topics in biblical studies. Some scholars see it as a local Mesopotamian flood event behind the text. Archaeological evidence from the Black Sea and Mesopotamian flood layers (Woolley, 1929) is cited by different sides.

sources.label: Walton & Longman — The Lost World of the Flood

events.title: Birth of Abraham
events.description: Abram born in Ur of the Chaldeans. Later renamed Abraham — father of many nations.
events.dateRationale: Derived by working back from the Exodus (c.1446 BC) using the 430 years of Exodus 12:40 and the genealogical data of Genesis 11. The date 2166 BC is widely used in evangelical chronologies (Archer, Merrill).
events.debate: Whether the patriarchal narratives reflect a specific historical period is debated. Kenneth Kitchen places Abraham in the Middle Bronze Age (2000–1500 BC) based on cultural parallels. John Van Seters argues for a much later composition with no historical core.

sources.label: Kitchen — On the Reliability of the Old Testament
Merrill — Kingdom of Priests: A History of Old Testament Israel

events.title: Abraham called from Ur
events.description: God calls Abram to leave Ur and travel to Canaan. The beginning of the Abrahamic covenant.
events.dateRationale: 75 years after Abraham's birth (Genesis 12:4), placing the call around 2091 BC in the standard evangelical chronology.
events.debate: The location of 'Ur of the Chaldeans' is itself debated — most scholars identify it with Tell el-Muqayyar in southern Iraq, but Cyrus Gordon argued for a northern Syrian Ur.

events.title: The Exodus from Egypt
events.description: Moses leads Israel out of Egypt following the ten plagues. The defining event of Israel's national identity.
events.dateRationale: The early date of 1446 BC is based on 1 Kings 6:1 which states Solomon's Temple was begun 480 years after the Exodus, plus the reference to the city of Ramesses in Exodus 1:11 (though this name may be anachronistic). The 15th century date aligns with the reign of Thutmose III or Amenhotep II.
events.debate: This is one of the most contested dates in biblical archaeology. The late date of c.1290 BC aligns with the reign of Ramesses II and is favoured by many archaeologists (Hoffmeier, Redford). The early date (1446 BC) is defended by Kitchen, Merrill and others. Some scholars (Finkelstein, Silberman) question whether a large-scale Exodus occurred at all, noting the absence of Egyptian records.

sources.label: Kitchen — On the Reliability of the Old Testament

events.title: Israel enters Canaan
events.description: Joshua leads Israel across the Jordan into the Promised Land after 40 years in the wilderness.
events.dateRationale: 40 years after the Exodus (Numbers 14:33), placing the conquest around 1406 BC on the early date scheme.
events.debate: The conquest narrative of Joshua has generated significant archaeological debate. The destruction layers at Jericho and Ai do not obviously correlate with a 15th century invasion. Bryant Wood has proposed revised dating of Jericho's destruction to fit the early date; others see the conquest as gradual infiltration rather than military campaign.

sources.label: Wood — Did the Israelites Conquer Jericho? A New Look at the Archaeological Evidence

events.title: Saul becomes first King
events.description: Israel demands a king. Saul anointed as Israel's first monarch by Samuel.
events.dateRationale: Derived from working back from the well-established date of Solomon's Temple (966 BC), accounting for the reigns of David (40 years) and Saul (40 years per Acts 13:21).
events.debate: The biblical account of the transition from judges to monarchy is studied extensively. Some scholars see the pro-monarchy and anti-monarchy traditions in 1 Samuel as reflecting later editorial tensions rather than historical narrative.

sources.label: Birch — The Rise of the Israelite Monarchy

events.title: David becomes King of Israel
events.description: David becomes king over all Israel. Jerusalem established as capital. The Davidic covenant promised an eternal dynasty.
events.dateRationale: One of the better-anchored dates in Israelite chronology. Working back from the Assyrian synchronisms which fix Ahab's death at 853 BC and using the Kings chronologies, David's reign is consistently placed around 1010–970 BC.
events.debate: The Tel Dan Inscription (1993) refers to the 'House of David' — the first extrabiblical mention of David, confirming he was a historical figure. However the scale of his empire as described in Samuel remains debated by archaeologists.

sources.label: Tel Dan Inscription — Biblical Archaeology Society

events.title: Solomon's Temple built
events.description: Solomon begins construction of the First Temple in Jerusalem — the permanent dwelling place for the Ark of the Covenant.
events.dateRationale: 1 Kings 6:1 states the Temple was begun in the 480th year after the Exodus and the 4th year of Solomon's reign. Using the Assyrian synchronisms to anchor Solomon's reign, this gives approximately 966 BC — one of the most agreed dates in Israelite chronology.
events.debate: The 480-year figure of 1 Kings 6:1 is either taken literally (supporting the early Exodus date of 1446 BC) or symbolically as 12 generations of 40 years (Bimson, Soggin). Archaeological evidence for a 10th century Jerusalem consistent with a major temple building project is debated.

sources.label: Mazar — Archaeology of the Land of the Bible
Galil — The Chronology of the Kings of Israel and Judah

events.title: Fall of Samaria — Northern Kingdom exiled
events.description: Assyria under Shalmaneser V and Sargon II captures Samaria. The ten northern tribes are deported — the origin of the 'Lost Tribes' tradition.
events.dateRationale: 722 BC is one of the most firmly established dates in biblical history, confirmed by multiple Assyrian annals — Sargon II's own records describe the deportation of 27,290 inhabitants of Samaria.
events.debate: The fate of the ten northern tribes has generated centuries of speculation. The 'British Israel' movement identified them with the British peoples; other theories suggest India, Ethiopia or Japan. Mainstream scholarship regards them as assimilated into Assyrian and later populations.

sources.label: Cogan — The Raging Torrent: Historical Inscriptions from Assyria and Babylonia

events.title: Jerusalem destroyed — Babylonian exile begins
events.description: Nebuchadnezzar destroys Solomon's Temple and Jerusalem. The population of Judah is deported to Babylon.
events.dateRationale: 586 BC is firmly anchored by the Babylonian Chronicles which record Nebuchadnezzar's campaigns. The destruction is independently confirmed by archaeological evidence at Lachish, Tel Batash and Jerusalem itself.
events.debate: Some scholars prefer 587 BC based on slightly different calculations of Babylonian regnal years. The difference of one year reflects debates about how Babylonian accession-year reckoning is translated into our calendar.

events.title: Cyrus conquers Babylon
events.description: Cyrus the Great of Persia conquers Babylon, ending the Neo-Babylonian Empire. Daniel witnesses the fall.
events.dateRationale: 539 BC is one of the most precisely dated events in ancient history, confirmed by the Nabonidus Chronicle and the Cyrus Cylinder. The date is universally agreed.
events.debate: The Cyrus Cylinder (British Museum) is often cited as confirming Cyrus's policy of allowing exiled peoples to return to their homelands, paralleling Ezra 1. Some scholars caution against reading Cyrus's decree as specifically directed at the Jews.

events.title: Birth of Jesus
events.description: Jesus born in Bethlehem of Judea during the reign of Herod the Great.
events.dateRationale: The birth must predate Herod's death, which is generally placed at 4 BC based on Josephus and astronomical data (lunar eclipse). This places Jesus's birth between 6 and 4 BC — before the 'Year 1' of the Christian calendar, which was miscalculated by Dionysius Exiguus in the 6th century.
events.debate: The date of Herod's death is itself debated. Filmer (1966) argued for 1 BC, which would allow a birth date of 1–2 BC. Most scholars accept 4 BC for Herod's death. The census of Luke 2 presents historical difficulties as no Roman census precisely matching Luke's description is documented for this period.

sources.label: Meier — A Marginal Jew: Rethinking the Historical Jesus

events.title: Crucifixion and Resurrection
events.description: Jesus crucified under Pontius Pilate. Rises on the third day. The central event of Christian faith.
events.dateRationale: Astronomical calculations show that Passover (14 Nisan) fell on a Friday in AD 30 and AD 33. Most scholars favour AD 30 or 33. The AD 30 date requires Jesus's ministry to begin around AD 27/28; AD 33 allows a longer ministry. Pilate was prefect AD 26–36, consistent with both dates.
events.debate: AD 30 is favoured by Hoehner, Dodd and most British scholarship. AD 33 is favoured by Humphreys & Waddington based on lunar eclipse calculations. The date of 14 Nisan vs 15 Nisan (Synoptics vs John) is a separate but related chronological problem.

sources.label: Humphreys & Waddington — The Jewish Calendar and the Date of the Crucifixion
Hoehner — Chronological Aspects of the Life of Christ

events.title: Destruction of Jerusalem
events.description: Roman general Titus destroys Jerusalem and the Second Temple. Over one million Jews killed according to Josephus. Fulfils Jesus's prophecy in Matthew 24.
events.dateRationale: AD 70 is one of the most precisely documented events in ancient history. Josephus gives a detailed eyewitness account. The Arch of Titus in Rome, erected AD 81, depicts the spoils including the Temple menorah.
events.debate: The destruction of the Temple is significant for New Testament dating — if the Gospels and Revelation were written after AD 70, the prophecies of Matthew 24 may be vaticinium ex eventu (prophecy after the fact). Conservative scholars date most NT books before AD 70 partly on these grounds.
